speaker: interviewer
text: --interview with Mr. Stefan Stok. Stefan, at the end of the last tape, you were talking about coming back to your flat, your apartment in Warsaw, and that there were many more members of family at the time--

speaker: interviewee
text: Living there.

speaker: interviewer
text: --in this flat.

speaker: interviewee
text: Living there. Yes, that's right. And it was not the only thing because the flat was as well-- where we lived was in the-- pretty much in the center of Warsaw and in the kind of smart center of Warsaw. There was a theater square where was the opera. It was a big theater really in the distance of one minute, and, and another square which is where-- which was a kind of a big, ceremonial square with palaces, and so only a distance of maybe two minutes. So it was very exposed place. And the result of that was, unfortunately, that we had-- that in this flat, we had quite a number of visits from the Germans already in this time in 1940. They would come, and they would look around. And they would requisition bits of furniture or bits of furniture or pictures or something like that. And they probably-- because we were-- and they knew that, that obviously, they had addresses of the Jewish families. And they, they came there a number of times and gave even my parents in the end, they took some furniture, and they took-- they requisitioned furniture. They requisitioned pictures. And they gave my mother in the end a receipt saying that it all is going-- that she, she will get those things back at the end of the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did she?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, they gave a receipt, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: No, did she get it back?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh but there was nothing at the end of the war, obviously.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did they take furniture or anything from a non-Jewish people or just from--

speaker: interviewee
text: I wouldn't-- I, I don't think so, not-- because those people who came, they were probably-- well, I don't know who they were exactly. They probably were connected to the Gestapo or something like it or the military. No, I think they were mostly took it from well-off Jewish families because which were whoever [INAUDIBLE] to say the most obvious victims of that particular policy. And that was-- but there was no ghetto at this time, and the-- we still lived in our place. And generally, there was-- but I think that everything was living full of rumors and uncertainty as far as the future was concerned. Nobody knew anything for certain, but I think there were already rumors that a ghetto was going to be opened because I think things like that already happened in probably in Lodz. In, in Lodz, it might have happened even earlier than in Warsaw. And so we-- I, I don't remember terribly clearly things from this early period in 1940. The one thing that I remember is that we weren't let-- on Pesach, on Passover of 1940, I-- we went to the house of my grandparents and--

speaker: interviewer
text: Maternal?

speaker: interviewee
text: Maternal grandparents, yes. There were no paternal grandparents because they both--

speaker: interviewer
text: Because they died before the war.

speaker: interviewee
text: --died before the war. And even maternal-- pater-- maternal grandparent, there was only grandfather because the grandmother remained in Bialystok and with one of my mother's sister, with Aunt Dora. And they both finished later in, in Siberia. And so she was not there. The grandmother was not there. The grandfather-- the grandfather was on his own, but they still probably some other aunts prepared Passover meal, which they made a traditional Passover meal. Which I remember that there was still the traditional Passover dishes with hard boiled eggs in salt, and-- which I actually thought was, was very nice. And the tradition of all the celebration of that, that is still a strong memory. And otherwise, I think there were, as I said, rumors of the time. And Jews were being brought to Warsaw from little towns around because once all those people already then coming with or pushing their little carts or with, with their possessions with all kinds of, of, of bits and pieces, mattresses and beds and so on. So they were already being brought from elsewhere. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: Why, why did they come? I mean--

speaker: interviewee
text: Because they were forced out of there-- because-- that was later. Became-- later in 1940, became-- and probably later in 1941, the Jews were concentrated in bigger places. That was one of the-- of the-- main, main things things, main points of the policy which later was an extermination policy. One had to concentrate them.

speaker: interviewer
text: So in Warsaw, Jews from little towns and villages around Warsaw in quite a big radius were brought there. I don't know really-- not from everywhere because obviously there were little towns near Warsaw where there were little ghettos on their own, like [NON-ENGLISH] I know. But quite a lot of were brought to Warsaw. And what happened was that-- what I remember is that I even wrote a little cartoon story all drawn and written by me about the adventures of a Jew who has to go to ghetto. That must have been later in 1940 about how it starts that he's-- that the-- the-- the Germans come to his flat and say, Juden raus. You understand what the word, yes? Yes, means Jews out.

speaker: interviewee
text: Out, And then he's getting his possessions together. And then he's-- this-- and he comes down and, and boys shout at him some kind of-- something like little Jew, just pejorative in Polish. And then he loses his things, and then he's beaten by the policeman and so on. And finally he goes to the ghetto, and he says, finally, I'm in the ghetto. So there was a certain-- although ghetto was feared, but as well there was a feeling that being--

speaker: interviewer
text: Of protection.

speaker: interviewee
text: --among other Jews was somehow safer and better. And that of course was felt later as well.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you were-- meanwhile, beforehand, you were at this apartment of yours? You were staying there-- for how long your family still stayed there?

speaker: interviewee
text: We stayed until autumn, 1940. And in the autumn, 1940, the ghetto was formed. Although it was formed, it was formed in such a way that the borders of the ghetto were announced in the newspaper. There was-- of course, there were newspapers in Warsaw, both German-speaking and Polish-speaking. But at this time, there must have been as well Yiddish newspapers. The borders were determined. But when it came to creating the-- forming the ghetto, months later, maybe, closing-- in other words, putting the first the barbed wire fencing and then the walls, it happened that it was really smaller than was originally announced. So immediately some areas, which were originally going to be inside, were taken out. And we were unlucky because I think-- well, we had to move because our apartment was outside the ghetto by quite a good distance. So we had to move.

speaker: interviewer
text: So my father found some apartment to rent in Tlomackie near this big synagogue. But then we found out that Tlomackie, after all, was not part of the ghetto. So we had to look for something else. And we finally found an apartment in Chlodna Street, Chlodna, I think, 49. And that was on the border of ghetto and the Aryan area. In fact, Chlodna was a street which was crossing the dividing between so-called big ghetto and small ghetto. And the-- when the-- although the pavements were in the ghetto, the road itself was not in the ghetto. It was part of the Aryan quarter. And we finally somehow moved into this apartment in-- probably in autumn, 1940.

speaker: interviewee
text: My grandfather died. That was my maternal grandfather, and that was a great shock. That was great, great shock for everybody. He was still not old. He was maybe in his early 60s. And that must have happened, I think, around Yom Kippur. I think what happened was that he went to his cousin, Hirsch Adler who-- whom I knew quite well was invited to for dinner and must have eaten too well and later, didn't feel well and had a heart attack. And he died. And that was-- of course, everybody was shocked. And that must have been then in probably September or beginning of October 1940. But in retrospect, it was quite lucky that it happened at this particular moment because it happened-- it was still in his own-- he was still brought to his flat. And it happened before any extreme measures were taken and before ghetto was formed. And as he was-- and as he was very religious orthodox Jew, it's almost unthinkable that he would have survived. So he was buried on the-- in the cemetery in Powazki, in the cemetery in Powazki. And there was a big funeral, which I don't-- I, I-- which I didn't go to. And so and then-- and that must have happened briefly before ghetto was-- before we went into the ghetto. Right. So when we moved-- I just try to think if there was anything else in this period, but it will come later. Right.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you-- so your family, it means your parents and yourself, or there were others that moved into this flat in this street in the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think it was-- I think it was only us. And there was, of course, a-- and so anyway, the question was of moving into the ghetto. People had decision to face whether to move into the ghetto or whether to try to stay in the Aryan side of Warsaw, pretending that one was Polish and pretending that one was Catholic and not Jewish at all. And that was, of course, thing which a number of very assimilated Jews did just that. They never moved into the ghetto. They stayed immediately because they were as well probably further seeing than the rest of us, and they could see that it was-- it ultimately was going to end--

speaker: interviewer
text: Bad-- --very badly. But for my parents, I don't think that it, even at this moment, it was an option. I don't think they even considered it at this moment. And they were as well-- the, the whole family moved into the ghetto and there was simply nobody even thought about it that one could have stayed outside the ghetto. It was obvious that one had to move into the ghetto. It was forced for Jews to move.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, one was forced, but as well, they didn't even consider the alternative of not going into the ghetto, of hiding in the Polish part of Warsaw. And the ghetto was formed. And what should I talk about? Should I talk about what to ghetto was or how it was?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah, just describe-- for example, describe the flat where you moved to.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, well, the flat was-- the flat, we still had some of the furniture from before the war, so some of the furniture and some of the furniture was gone, as I explained. But some furniture still remained and we got some of that furniture into that flat. The flat was not particularly beautiful. It was darkish in a typical Warsaw apartment block, which was mainly apartment blocks built around the courtyard. There was a central courtyard and the flat and the block of-- the apartment block formed a kind of a rectangle around the courtyard. And the courtyard was reached by a entrance hall from the street. You went into the-- there was a big gate at the-- in the street. And then through that gate, you came into a kind of vaulting into a kind of a little tunnel and there usually was the entrance to the main staircase, the staircase leading to the apartments, which were in the front part of the house, out facing the street. Those were usually the best apartments. And then you walk through this vaulting, you came to a large, large courtyard. And the houses were-- and the house, the block was in a rectangle around the courtyard with usually something like maybe three or four staircases, on each side, a staircase. And on the staircase in the back, and each staircase went up five or six floors and, and, and, and on each floor, you had three or four apartments, entered from the-- from the common hall. And there was a lot of life happening in the courtyard. In the courtyard, people were coming and going. And all kinds of beggars, or street beggars were coming there and playing music. And later, children in ghetto-- children would come as well and say their-- their things, ask for, for, for charity. And well, carpets were, were, were cleaned and, and so on. And that was-- and this apartment was not beautiful. I remember dark and our-- and our-- and our furniture still from before the war, some of it, at least. But it was, of course, something fantastic to have your own flat in the-- your own furniture and your own flat in the ghetto. And we still continued--

speaker: interviewer
text: Why was it-- why was it lucky to have your own furniture?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well it was lucky because, because, because people were living already-- at this stage in the ghetto, the majority of the population were-- if they were lucky, they had one room--

speaker: interviewer
text: For whole family?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, for the whole family. And if they were unlucky-- of course, it helped that we were from Warsaw and that we still had some money. People who were coming without anything from outside Warsaw, particularly those that were coming from little towns around, they had nothing. And they were coming, and they were put in a kind of a mass houses, which were-- there were some of them which were called points, [NON-ENGLISH] And they had to live there maybe whole family to a room or maybe even more than one whole family to a room. And they had-- and there was-- and they had, of course, probably no food and then the food supply was-- the official food supply was never enough to keep one alive. And then they had-- and there was no heating and people were spending-- in those places if they were able to warm themselves, it was only by a staying in bed. So some of them were staying all day in bed covering all possible covers and so on just to keep warm. And but not everybody could stay in bed because they had as well to go and look for some way of, of, of keeping their body together. So they had to look for to try to, to, to, trade or to buy and sell something. And in winter, it's very hard.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the conditions in ghetto immediately in I think in that winter, in 1940, '41 were probably even worse than they were later because it was winter, because there was a huge amount of people squeezed into those-- into the ghetto. I mean, that area of Warsaw where the ghetto was, that was a central part-- north-- central north part of Warsaw, which was already before the war Jewish quarter, a Jewish area. And it Must have been a Jewish area for a long time.

speaker: interviewee
text: And it was-- most of it was tightly built together. Those were those big apartment blocks of five stories, six stories, all clo-- all closely together all around those courtyards. And some of them, if you went to in Nalewki, which was the main street, one of the main streets, of the ghetto, some of them were-- there was not one courtyard, but there were two, three, four courtyards. And there were maybe more than 1,000 people living in such one apartment block. And they were not only living there. They were as well working there because quite a lot of population of, of, of ghetto were-- particularly those who lived in Warsaw already before the war-- were living from small-- for-- they were-- had small workshops. And that was the kind of-- that was-- generally, the large part of the Jewish population of Warsaw was poor, really poor. They had-- they managed to survive quite well before the war by, by, by doing-- working very hard and very menial trades very often. And lots of them were doing things like-- they were doing like probably-- well, no one does in-- or maybe not anymore-- in Hong Kong or something. They were doing things like, like doing clothes, and probably a family would be doing bits of clothes or some particular part of it in their own little flat. They would be having a workshop there and working very hard. So there was already-- so that part of Warsaw was already before the war tightly populated. There were probably-- I don't know how many, but there must have been there maybe quarter of a million in-- in that area, something like that. But now it was possibly almost doubled. It was probably in the winter of 1940, '41, they might have been up to-- there were more than 400,000. They were maybe closer to half a million. That was because all those people were brought from other parts of Warsaw to start and from the outside Warsaw, from the towns and villages around Warsaw. So it really was-- ghetto was--

speaker: interviewer
text: Condensed.

speaker: interviewee
text: --a really crowded place. You went in a street, and the street was crowded. People were everywhere. You couldn't pass in the pavement, particularly in the main streets. You-- the pavements were full. The roads were full. There was no transport in the-- there were no cars unless there were some German car belonged to the Germans passing by. But otherwise, I don't think there were any cars. There was a tram, but even the electric trams were-- which were passing through the ghetto, were not stopping and not to be used by the Jews. So there were trams which-- then in the ghetto itself, there was transport which was arranged all by big and small way by the Jews. There were carts. There were some horse carts, I think, but not so many, I think.

speaker: interviewer
text: So why did the tram pass through the ghetto if it didn't stop there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because it was-- no, it didn't stop. It was probably still the old line which was passing from south Warsaw to north Warsaw. And they still had it, it passing by, but it wouldn't stop. But there were trams then used by-- well, there were private companies. They started having trams which were used in the ghetto, and they were horse trams. They were coaches. So to say it was a kind of a large wagon painted, I think, white and blue, if I remember correctly, with two horses. And they were-- there were a few lines like that inside the ghetto. But I think not all that many people went in the trams. There was, however, a lot of rickshaws. There were just rickshaws which were cycle rickshaws, which was a little open cart with the kind of an armchair with places for three people, maybe, sitting one alongside another with bicycle wheels to, to a tricycle with bicycle wheels and a-- and a wheel behind, and the rickshaw, which was-- which was the most-- as I remember, it was the most common way of transport. But the--

speaker: interviewer
text: And who was carrying this? Who carried the rickshaw?

speaker: interviewee
text: Who pu--

speaker: interviewer
text: Who pulled it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Who drove the rickshaw? The rickshaw driver. There was the man who-- it was like a taxi. It was the way-- it was the taxi. And apart from that, there were carts. People were pushing things on carts, on wheelbarrows, and so on. So practically, it was a completely-- there was this impression of crowd. There was crowd everywhere, crowd on the roads, crowd on the pavements, masses and masses of people. Then they had as well there were all kinds of stands which were-- there were-- people were selling things, you know, like selling something to eat or their own possessions and so on. And the conditions were really very, very difficult and crowded. And of course, the Germans, when they started the ghetto, they-- the official reasoning for that, what they said, was that the Jews were the carriers of the typhoid, and the Jews were unhygienic. They were dirty. They were-- they were danger to the rest of the population. And one had to isolate them, and in this way, one would be able to isolate the danger of epidemics.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. We're coming now to the end of tape number three. Thank you.
